Distinct real-root count for 4-fold iterate hitting a critical value

Let $P(x)=x^{3}-3x+1$. Define the 4-fold iterate $P^{(4)}(x)=P(P(P(P(x))))$.
How many **distinct** real numbers $x$ satisfy
$$
P^{(4)}(x)=3?
$$

Solution.
Step 1 (critical points/values and how many real preimages a value has).
Compute
$$
P'(x)=3x^{2}-3=3(x-1)(x+1),
so the critical points are $x=\pm1$. The corresponding critical values are
P(-1)=(-1)^3-3(-1)+1=3,\qquad P(1)=1-3+1=-1.
$$
Since $P$ is increasing on $(-\infty,-1]$, decreasing on $[-1,1]$, and increasing on $[1,\infty)$, the horizontal line $y=\alpha$ meets the cubic in:
- exactly 3 distinct real points if $-1<\alpha<3$,
- exactly 1 real point if $\alpha<-1$ or $\alpha>3$,
- exactly 2 distinct real points if $\alpha\in\{-1,3\}$ (one of them is a double root at a critical point).

Step 2 (localize the preimages and record the "type transitions").
For $-1<\alpha<3$, use the values
$$
P(-2)=-1,\quad P(-1)=3,\quad P(1)=-1,\quad P(2)=3
$$
together with monotonicity on each interval to conclude:
- one solution of $P(x)=\alpha$ lies in $(-2,-1)$, hence is $< -1$;
- one lies in $(-1,1)$, hence is in $(-1,3)$;
- one lies in $(1,2)$, hence is in $(-1,3)$.
Thus for $\alpha\in(-1,3)$, the set of distinct real preimages consists of:
$$
1\text{ value }< -1 \quad\text{and}\quad 2\text{ values in }(-1,3).
$$
For the boundary values:
- If $\alpha=3$, then $P(x)=3$ has distinct solutions $x=-1$ (double) and $x=2$.
- If $\alpha=-1$, then $P(x)=-1$ has distinct solutions $x=1$ (double) and $x=-2$.

Introduce three "types" of real values:
$$
\text{Type A: } \alpha<-1,\qquad
\text{Type B: } -1<\alpha<3,\qquad
\text{Type C: } \alpha=-1,
$$
and treat the target value $\alpha=3$ as a special start state.

The distinct-preimage transitions are:
- From start $\alpha=3$: preimages are $-1$ (Type C) and $2$ (Type B).
- Type C ($\alpha=-1$) has 2 distinct preimages: one Type A (namely $-2$) and one Type B (namely $1$).
- Type B has 3 distinct preimages: $1$ Type A and $2$ Type B.
- Type A has 1 real preimage, and it is again Type A (because if $\alpha<-1$, the unique solution lies on $(-\infty,-1)$, hence is $<-1$).

Step 3 (set up the recurrence for counts and compute up to the 4th preimage level).
Let $A_n,B_n,C_n$ be the numbers of Type A, Type B, Type C values at "level $n$", where:
- level 0 is the single target value $3$,
- level $n$ is the set of distinct real solutions of $P^{(n)}(x)=3$.

Level 1 (solutions to $P(x)=3$) is $\{-1,2\}$, i.e.
$$
(C_1,B_1,A_1)=(1,1,0).
$$

Level 2: take distinct preimages of each level-1 value.
- From the Type C node $-1$: contributes $1$ Type A and $1$ Type B.
- From the Type B node $2$: contributes $1$ Type A and $2$ Type B.
Hence
$$
(A_2,B_2,C_2)=(2,3,0).
$$
From here on, $C_n=0$ forever, because a value $-1$ can only appear as a preimage of $3$, and $3$ only occurs at level 0.

So for $n\ge 2$ we have the two-type recurrence:
$$
A_{n+1}=A_n+B_n,\qquad B_{n+1}=2B_n,
$$
with $(A_2,B_2)=(2,3)$.

Compute:
$$
(A_3,B_3)=(A_2+B_2,\,2B_2)=(2+3,\,6)=(5,6),
$$
$$
(A_4,B_4)=(A_3+B_3,\,2B_3)=(5+6,\,12)=(11,12).
$$

Therefore the number of distinct real solutions of $P^{(4)}(x)=3$ is
$$
|A_4|+|B_4|=11+12=23.
$$

Step 4 (why summing branch counts equals the number of distinct real roots).
At each level, we take unions of sets of solutions to $P(x)=\alpha$ for distinct $\alpha$ from the previous level. These preimage sets are disjoint because $P$ is a function: a real $x$ cannot satisfy $P(x)=\alpha_1$ and $P(x)=\alpha_2$ with $\alpha_1\ne\alpha_2$. Thus the count at each level is exactly the sum of the counts contributed by each node, and the recurrence above counts **distinct** real solutions (even though some of them may occur with multiplicity as roots of $P^{(4)}(x)-3$).

Answer: $\boxed{23}$.

Uniqueness Test
1) (Deterministic branching) The critical-value analysis uniquely determines, for each real $\alpha$, whether $P(x)=\alpha$ has 1, 2, or 3 distinct real solutions, and where those solutions lie. Hence the transition rules "A\toA", "B\toA+2B", "C\toA+B", and "3\toC+B" are forced and do not depend on any choices.

2) (No overcounting) For distinct $\alpha_1\ne\alpha_2$, the solution sets $P^{-1}(\alpha_1)$ and $P^{-1}(\alpha_2)$ are disjoint. Therefore the number of distinct real preimages at each step is uniquely the sum over nodes of their distinct-preimage counts.

3) (Closure of types after level 2) Since the only way to produce the special value $-1$ as a node is to take a preimage of $3$, and $3$ occurs only at the target level, one has $C_n=0$ for all $n\ge2$. Thus from level 2 onward the pair $(A_n,B_n)$ evolves by the unique linear recurrence
$$
A_{n+1}=A_n+B_n,\quad B_{n+1}=2B_n
$$
with uniquely determined initial condition $(A_2,B_2)=(2,3)$. Hence $(A_4,B_4)$ and the total $A_4+B_4=23$ are unique.

23
